coredata.dc:title: Novel Damsissa carbon quantum dots (DCQDs) to improve the protection efficiency of permanganate phosphate conversion coats (PPC) on steel
coredata.dc:creator: Abd-El-Nabey, B.A.
Mahmoud, Mohamed E.
El-Housseiny, S.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are spherical zero dimensional nanoparticles, generally below 10 nm in size, which received increasing attention in diverse application fields owing to their important characteristics and advantages as good water solubility, potential of environmental protection and preparation from abundant and sustainable raw materials. In this work, Damsissa carbon quantum dots (DCQDs) were generated via hydrothermal reaction microwaves irradiation. The DCQDs material was characterized by FTIR, TEM, EDX, XRD, and XPS to confirm the morphological and functionality aspects as well as particle size distribution, elemental composition, crystallite structure, and elemental identification in terms of chemical state. The FTIR analysis referred to associated assignments of C-H, C=O, C=C, C-N, besides other groups. The TEM study confirmed a ranged homogenous particle distribution at 3-6 nm. The EDX and XPS results verified carbon, nitrogen and oxygen as the main elements in DCQDs. The protection efficiency performance of permanganate phosphate conversion coat (PPC) was investigated and monitored by using different concentrations of DCQDs in the coating bath utilizing the potentiodynamic polarization technique, besides electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The results referred to 97.4% maximum protection efficiency for the coat in presence of 400 mg/L DCQDs. The impedance results showed that as the concentration of DCQDs increase, the capacity of the double layer (Cdl) decreases due to the adsorption ofDamsissa carbon dots on the steel surface. However, the potentiodynamic polarization results indicated that the presence of DCQDs in HCl solution could lead to the polarization of the cathodic and anodic curves to denote that these retard the cathodic and anodic reactions at the steel surface inhibiting the corrosion process. The coats synthesized in absence and in presence of diverse amounts of DCQDs were examined by the TEM, EDX, XPS, and XRD studies to elucidate the role of DCQDs in the improvement of the corrosion protection of the coat. The results indicated that the DCQDs molecules ofDamsissa were adsorbed at the steel surface to efficiently retard the dissolution of iron and decrease the Fe2+ ions concentration in the coating solution causing higher nucleation rate over the crystal growth and thus leading to the formation of a uniform protective film.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon Quantum Dots
Steel
Conversion Coating
Characterization
Electrochemical Techniques
Surface Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Coat stability with various immersion periods
Role of DCQDs in the mechanism of coat formation on the steel surface
Comparison of DCQDs performance versus other CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Related spectra to DCQDs (A) XRD, (B) FTIR, and (C) FS.
TEM image (A), EDX (B) and particle size distribution (C).
(A) XPS spectrum of the DCQDs and high-resolution deconvoluted profiles of (B) C 1s and (C) O 1s.
XRD of (A) steel, (B) PPC coated steel (C) PPC coated steel in presence of 100 mg/L DCQDs and (D) PPC coated steel in presence of 400 mg/L DCQDs. All samples were treated with 1.0 M-HCl for 1 h.

XPS spectra of (A) PPC coated steel, (B) PPC coated steel treated with 1.0 M-HCl for 1 h, (C) PPC coated steel with 100 mg/L DCQDs treated with 1.0 M-HCl for 1 h and (D) PPC coated steel with 400 mg/L DCQDs treated with 1.0 M-HCl for 1 h.
TEM of (A) steel coated with PPC, (B) steel coated with PPC in presence of 100 mg/L DCQDs and (C) steel coated with PPC in presence of 400 mg/L DCQDs. All after treatment with 1.0 M-HCl for 1 h.
EDX of (A) steel coated with PPC, (B) steel coated with PPC in presence of 100 mg/L DCQDs and (C) steel coated with PPC in presence of 400 mg/L DCQDs. All after treatment with 1.0 M-HCl for 1 h.
Nyquist plots of PPC coated steel in 1.0 M-HCl solution (coats contain different concentrations of DCQDs).
Equivalent circuit of coated steel and computer fitting of equivalent circuit of Nyquist for PPC coated steel contain 400 mg/L in 1.0 M-HCl solution.
Polarization curves of PPC coated steel with different DCQDs concentration in 1.0 M-HCl
DCQDs concentration impact on the efficiency of protection for PPC coats on steel in M-HCl
Nyquist plots of PPC coated steel in 1.0 M-HCl solution (coats contain 400 mg/L of DCQDs) at different immersion times.
Schematic representation of the mechanism of conversion coating.
The Thickness values of the conversion-coated steel samples in presence of different concentration of each of DCQDs.
Hardness of uncoated steel substrate and coated steel with and without 400 mg/L.

row.entry: PPC coated steel containing 400 mg/L DCQDs

row.entry: PPC coated steel containing 400 mg/L DCQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: EIS parameters of PPC coated steel in 1.0 M-HCl in and out of various DCQDs concentrations at 30 OC.
Electrochemical parameters of PPC coated steel in 1.0 M-HCl in presence of different amounts of DCQDs at 30 OC.
EIS parameters of PPC coated steel in 1.0 M-HCl solution (coats contain 400 mg/L of DCQDs) at different immersion times at 30 °C.
Comparison of DCQDs results with other carbon dots for steel in 1.0 M-HCl.

row.entry: N-doped carbon dots from aminosalicylic acid (ASA)
N-doped carbon dots from p-phenylenediamine (p-PD) and ophenylenediamine (o-PD)
N-doped carbon dots from citric acid, diethylenetriamine, and urotropine
Functionalized carbon dots from citric acid, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride, N-carboxysuccinimide, N-(3-aminopropyl) imidazole, triethylamine
N-doped carbon dots from ammonium citrate
N-doped carbon dots from methacrylic acid and n-butylamine
N,S-co-doped carbon dots from ammonium citrate and methionine
N-doped carbon quantum dots from 4-amino salicylic acid and Lhistidine

head.ce:title: Novel Damsissa carbon quantum dots (DCQDs) to improve the protection efficiency of permanganate phosphate conversion coats (PPC) on steel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aFaculty of Science, Chemistry Department, Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Jouf University, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bFaculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Jouf University, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Science, Chemistry Department, Kuwait University, Kuwait
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cFaculty of Science, Chemistry Department, Kuwait University, Kuwait

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Sciences, Chemistry Department, Jouf University, Saudi Arabia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are spherical zero dimensional nanoparticles, generally below 10 nm in size, which received increasing attention in diverse application fields owing to their important characteristics and advantages as good water solubility, potential of environmental protection and preparation from abundant and sustainable raw materials. In this work, Damsissa carbon quantum dots (DCQDs) were generated via hydrothermal reaction microwaves irradiation. The DCQDs material was characterized by FTIR, TEM, EDX, XRD, and XPS to confirm the morphological and functionality aspects as well as particle size distribution, elemental composition, crystallite structure, and elemental identification in terms of chemical state. The FTIR analysis referred to associated assignments of C-H, C=O, C=C, C-N, besides other groups. The TEM study confirmed a ranged homogenous particle distribution at 3-6 nm. The EDX and XPS results verified carbon, nitrogen and oxygen as the main elements in DCQDs. The protection efficiency performance of permanganate phosphate conversion coat (PPC) was investigated and monitored by using different concentrations of DCQDs in the coating bath utilizing the potentiodynamic polarization technique, besides electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. The results referred to 97.4% maximum protection efficiency for the coat in presence of 400 mg/L DCQDs. The impedance results showed that as the concentration of DCQDs increase, the capacity of the double layer (Cdl) decreases due to the adsorption ofDamsissa carbon dots on the steel surface. However, the potentiodynamic polarization results indicated that the presence of DCQDs in HCl solution could lead to the polarization of the cathodic and anodic curves to denote that these retard the cathodic and anodic reactions at the steel surface inhibiting the corrosion process. The coats synthesized in absence and in presence of diverse amounts of DCQDs were examined by the TEM, EDX, XPS, and XRD studies to elucidate the role of DCQDs in the improvement of the corrosion protection of the coat. The results indicated that the DCQDs molecules ofDamsissa were adsorbed at the steel surface to efficiently retard the dissolution of iron and decrease the Fe2+ ions concentration in the coating solution causing higher nucleation rate over the crystal growth and thus leading to the formation of a uniform protective film.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is a widely used material with a variety of applications due to excellent cost-effectiveness and mechanical properties [1–4]. The primary limitation of carbon steel is related to susceptibility to corrosion, especially in environments where moisture, salts, or chemicals are present [5–8]. To protect carbon steel against corrosion, various methods are generally employed, and therefore, conversion coatings are indeed one of the effective techniques [9]. Conversion coatings create a chemically treated surface layer on the metal, which helps to inhibit corrosion. However, other common conversion coating methods for carbon steel may include phosphating, chromate conversion coating, passivation, zinc coatings, black oxide coating and rust converters [10]. Permanganate phosphate (PP) coatings are applied to various metals as carbon steel, to improve their corrosion resistance [11,12]. The coating bath contains a combination of chemicals that work together to form a PP conversion coating on metal substrates. Potassium permanganate acts as a powerful oxidizing agent, while the phosphate compounds contribute to the coating formation and performance [13]. Phosphoric acid is used to regulate the solution pH, ensuring that the process proceeds effectively to produce a corrosion-resistant coating. This type of coating is used to increase the durability and resistance to corrosion of metal surfaces, particularly in applications where protection against environmental factors is crucial [14]. The use of PPC coating bath with the addition of surfactants was previously studied and reported to improve and enhance the resistance to corrosion of the coating on steel surface by using 0.6 M-NH4NO3 solution [15]. This study employed various analytical techniques to assess the coatings effectiveness and investigate the interactions between the surfactants and metallic surface. Adsorption reaction of these surfactants onto metal surface was characterized as a key factor in enhancing the protection efficiency of conversion coat [16].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or carbon nanodots, are tiny, quasi-spherical nanoparticles made primarily of carbon. They typically have size range of few nanometers to few tens nanometers [17–19]. These nanoscale carbon structures are known by their chemical properties and unique optical performance [20]. They are composed of carbon with various surface functionalities as amino and carboxylic groups. CQDs are extremely small, which gives them quantum confinement effects. Their atomic structure can vary, but they are generally amorphous or contain tiny crystalline domains [21]. Carbon dots exhibit strong photoluminescence, meaning that they can emit visible light after excitation by ultraviolet or visible light. This property has made them popular for applications in imaging, sensing, and optoelectronics [22]. Carbon dots have found applications in various fields, including bioimaging, drug delivery, sensors, and photocatalysis [23–25]. These were found great interest due to their low toxicity and biocompatibility, making them suitable for biological applications [26]. Carbon dots can be synthesized by different methods, viz. chemical oxidation of carbon-containing precursors like glucose or citric acid, laser ablation, and microwave-assisted processes. CQDs have gained great attentions as potential green inhibitors due to their remarkable corrosion protection properties and the following are some notable findings from research works in this field. Cui et al [18] synthesized CQDs and demonstrated their effectiveness as inhibitors for corrosion of carbon steel. They achieved 96% impressive corrosion inhibition rate, highlighting the excellent performance of CQDs in acidic environments. Ye and co-workers [27] reported a step further by synthesizing N-CQDs, which contain nitrogen elements. It was found that N-CQDs more effective in protecting against metal corrosion compared to traditional CQDs without nitrogen. Zhu and his team [28] prepared N,S-CQDs by using thiourea and o-phenylenediamine to refer that the presence of both sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) significantly enhanced the efficiency of CQDs in inhibiting corrosion. These studies collectively demonstrated the vital role played by the heteroatoms in increasing the corrosion inhibition efficiency of CQDs. Lupine carbon quantum dots was synthesized, characterized and investigated as an effective, sustainable and superior inhibitor target for carbon steel corrosion reflecting a maximum effectiveness (89.3 %) [29]. In a previous work, the efficiency of Ambrosia maritime, L. (Damsissa) plant extract was testified to inhibit the steel corrosion in 1.0 M-H2SO4 referring that the inhibition was concentration-dependent and more effective at lower temperatures. The research also provided insights into the mechanism of adsorption and activation parameters contributing to the understanding of the corrosion inhibition process [30].

In this work, Damssisa was implemented as a biomass material for preparation of carbon quantum dots (DCQDs) to improve the protection efficiency of PP coat on steel surface. This approach is categorized with several advantages. i) Damssisa contains significant organic substances, such as lactones, phenolic compounds, essential oils, alkaloids, and pollen allergens. These compounds serve as doping elements and carbon sources for the synthesis of DCQDs. ii) Damssisa has low toxicity, making a safe source and eco-friendly material for the production of DCQDs. iii) Damssisa is widely available and relatively inexpensive to make the synthesis of BCQDs a cost-effective approach, iv) Derived DCQDs from Damssisa are biocompatible and can be advantageous when considering potential applications in industries where biocompatibility is essential, such as in the biomedical field. Therefore, DCQDs material was successfully obtained from Damsissa plant via microwave hydrothermal generation reaction. The current study involves a comprehensive characterization of the synthesized DCQDs material using various techniques as FTIR, TEM, EDX, XPS, and XRD techniques to afford insights into the morphology and composition of DCQDs. The protection capability of prepared coats was monitored by potentiodynamic polarization measurement and EIS techniques. To investigate the role of DCQDs in improving the corrosion resistance of PPC coatings, monitoring of the coats in absence and presence of DCQDs via TEM, EDX, XRD techniques. Therefore, the novelty in this wok can be summarized by the new eco-friendly preparation of Damsissa carbon quantum dots from Damsissa plant and implementing the synthesized DCQDs in improving and enhancing the protection performance of the surface PP coatings.

Steel material was employed as the working electrode with the following elemental composition in weight percentage of S (0.04%), P (0.04%), C (0.21%), Si (0.35%), Mn (2.5%), and the remaining element is Fe to apply in the electrochemical corrosion study. The construction of the electrode of steel in a cylindrical shape, covered in Teflon with only one exposed surface (1 cm²), is a practical approach to prevent the crevice effect in electrochemical experiments. Steel panels of dimensions 5 cm × 2 cm × 0.2 cm were used. Other chemicals as 85% H3PO4, 37% HCl, NaHPO4, and KMnO4 from Aldrich chemicals of high purity were employed to ensure the reliability of the stock solutions. The detailed experimental setup reflects the importance of precise and controlled conditions in electrochemical investigations, particularly when studying corrosion resistance and protective coatings on metal surfaces.

Damsissa (Ambrosia maritima, L.) family composite (Asteraceae), is widely grown in Egypt, especially in Sinai. It is a common folk medicine used in the treatment of rheumatic pains, asthma, bilharziasis, and diabetes, stomachic [31]. The chemical constituents of plant contain on Damsin, Ambrosin, and Coumarin compounds, with compounds contains oxygen, nitrogen atoms and π-electrons bonds as shown in our previous work [32].

A microwave irradiation reaction was applied to yield the aimed DCQDs from the low-cost Damsissa plant source via a one-step pyrolysis procedure. The plant was repeatedly purified by using double distilled water (DDW), dried and grinded by a mixer. Damsissa powder (18.0 g) was added to DDW (120 mL) and further heated in the microwave (at 700-800 W) for 15 min. The solution was converted into brown color to refer to DCQDs production. After cooling, DCQDs material was mixed with 150 mL DDW for 30 min to finally obtain in an aqueous suspension. The DCQDs material was filtered by regular filter paper and followed by superfine filtration using 0.22 µm filter paper [30].

The optical measurements of DCQDs were accomplished by a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 UV spectrometer and a Hitachi F-2700 fluorescence spectrometer. The structural analysis of DCQDs involved FTIR by a Nicolet iS50 instrument from Thermo and XRD using a Bruker-AXS/D8 ECO instrument. The content, species, and chemical composition of BCQDs were studied by XPS using an ESCALAB250Xi instrument. Additionally, elemental composition, distribution of the particle size, and morphology of DCQDs were tested by TEM and EDX using Talos F200S microscope, Thermo Fisher Scientific.

The solution for conversion coating was obtained by mixing 0.75 g NaHPO4, 1.0 g KMnO4, 2 mL H3PO4, DCQDs solutions, and double distilled water in a 100 mL flask. The concentration of DCQDs stock solution is 800 mg/L in 250 ml. A certain volume of DCQDs solutions was added to achieve the required DCQDs concentration. The coating solution was adjusted to pH 3 and the mixture was diluted with DDW to reach the required volume. The coating procedure involved (i) pre-treatment in which the working electrode was hand-polished using the emery paper ranging from 400 to 1000 grit. Start with a coarse and gradually move to finer, achieving a mirror finish. Conduct pickling using a method reported in a previous work [15]. (ii) Main coating process in which the pre-treated working electrode was introduced into the prepared coating solution. The working electrode is suspended by a rotator at 60 rotations per minute and the electrode was then immersed into coating solution (30 min).

In this experimental procedure, one-square-centimeter steel coupons were employed. These coupons were immersed in a test solution coat at different concentrations of DCQDs for up to 1 hour. After the designated immersion time, the coupons of steel were removed. The retrieved coupons were dried. XPS, EDX, XRD, and TEM were used to examine the morphology of coupons surface. This experimental setup and analysis sequence can provide a comprehensive investigation into the surface film formed on the coupons in the presence of DCQDs, to collect valuable insights into the material interactions and the effectiveness of DCQDs under various concentrations and exposure durations. In addition, the dry film thickness (PosiTest DFT Ferrous) or coating thickness was measured.

Nanoindentations tests were performed on all samples using a Hysitron Triboindenter TI950 (Bruker, Billerica, MA, USA) equipped with Berkovic probe. A maximum force of 10 mN was applied, and the hardness values were measured over ten different locations on the sample.

Pull off adhesion test was performed according to ASTM D4541 standard method by using an adhesion tester (Elcometer 106, USA). A pull stub (dolly) was fixed on the surface of coated samples using an adhesive resin (Loctite 495, USA). A tensile load with a rate of 3.8 MPa.s−1 was applied to the dolly by a loading fixture. The tensile force was increased smoothly and symmetrically, normal to the surface of the coated sample. Under tensile loading, the dolly could have detached from the surface either at the dolly-adhesive or coating-substrate interface suggesting the failure of adhesive or the coating respectively. Coating adhesion strength was evaluated by the pull-off test corresponding to the coating failure that occurred at the coating-substrate interface.

In the electrochemical measurements, a carefully designed setup was employed. A 100 mL cell was utilized for electrochemical measurements. Three electrodes were employed in a typical three-electrode cell configuration. The working electrode faced the counter electrode, while the reference electrode was selected as the saturated calomel. The coats protection efficiency versus DCQDs concentration using the identified results from both EIS and potentiodynamic polarization are shown in Fig. 11. The curves show that the three techniques gave similar results.

Electrochemical experiments were conducted by a PARSTAT 2263.02 SN 194 frequency response analyzer potentiostat instrument. This instrument was specifically designed for electrochemical analysis. Before initiating the electrochemical experiments, a 0.2826 cm² working electrode was immersed in a 1.0 M-HCl for 30 minutes at 30 °C to establish the rest potential. Potentiodynamic polarization investigation was conducted at a 20 mV/min rate using a cathodic potential (Ecorr -300 mV) and progressing in the anodic direction (Ecorr +900 mV). In the EIS experiments, the impedance spectrum was measured over a frequency range at 3.2 × 104 - 0.1 Hz with 10 mV amplitude across rest potential. To confirm result consistency, all experiments were repeated twice with the identical conditions to provide no more than 2% deviation. This detailed experimental approach ensures reliable and reproducible electrochemical data [17,18].

The XRD pattern of DCQDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 (A) to indicate that the DCQDs exhibited a graphene-like structure. The peak at 2θ = 20∘ corresponds to carbon moiety as detected in graphene-like structures [31]. Such broad diffraction peak suggests that the DCQDs material is naturally amorphous due to the influence of carbonaceous groups or doped O and N elements in DCQDs.

Fig. 1(B) represents the FTIR analysis of DCQDs in which a broad peak at 3500-3200 cm−1 which is ascribed to both O-H and N-H functionalities. Such broad peak may be correlated to internal hydrogen bonds between various functionalities in DCQDs as –COOH, O-H and N-H groups. The 2925 cm−1 and 1162 cm−1 peaks are assigned to the C-H and C=O vibrations, respectively. The 1458 cm−1 peak corresponds for C=C in benzene ring, while the 1376 cm−1 and 1234 cm−1 peaks are associated with C-N and C=O, respectively. The 1000−1458 cm−1 range refers to C-H in-plane and C=O vibrations, while the range 488−1000 cm−1 for C-H out-plane vibrations. Overall, the FTIR results suggest that DCQDs material is loaded with numerous N and O functional groups, as well as surface unsaturated double bonds. These groups are expected to contribute to the potential metal corrosion inhibition capabilities of DCQDs.

Fig. 1(C) shows the fluorescence spectra (FS) of DCQDs. The presence of two emission peaks in the fluorescence spectra of DCQDs, along with the correlation between emission and excitation wavelengths, indicates the existence of distinct photoluminescent properties. Specifically, the emission wavelengths are observed at 395 to 446 nm which are associated with different excitation wavelengths, viz. 316 nm for the first set and 248 nm for the second set. The UV-induced fluorescence is produced when exposed to UV illumination at 345 nm, and therefore, the DCQDs solution emits green fluorescence light. This UV-induced fluorescence further emphasizes the photoluminescent characteristics of the DCQDs. These findings align with the photoluminescence properties commonly observed in carbon quantum dots, where the emission wavelengths depend on the structure, size, and surface functionalities in DCQDs [32]. The ability of DCQDs to emit light under UV illumination, especially in the green spectrum, indicates their potential applicability in fluorescence imaging, sensing, or other optoelectronic devices. The observed photoluminescence properties were studied and reported to support the understanding of DCQDs optical characteristics [33].

The TEM-EDX analysis of as-prepared DCQDs in this study can provide an important characterization tool to conclude detailed insights into their morphology, composition, and particle size distribution. Fig. 2 (A) refers to the distribution of DCQDs in a uniform spherical shape as concluded from the TEM image. This suggests a unique structural arrangement of the quantum dots. The observed morphology supports the formation of DCQDs with distinctive features that could influence their properties. The elemental composition as deduced from the EDX analysis as represented in Fig. 2(B) to confirm that the DCQDs are composed of carbon (87.76 %), oxygen (5.29 %), nitrogen (2.82 %), sulfur (1.81 %), potassium (1.39 %), and silicon (1.02 %) elements. These results confirm the effective doping of some heteroatoms as N, S, Si and K from the biomass precursors into the assembled DCQDs. Finally, the estimated particle size distribution is provided in Fig. 2(C) to confirm the existence of most DCQDs particles in the range from 3 to 4 nm (25 %), 4 to 5 nm (35 %), 5 to 6 nm (40 %) and thus providing 4.65 nm as the average particle size of DCQDs material. Such particle size is suggested to exhibit a denser DCQDs protective layer on the steel surface compared to conventional organic corrosion inhibitors.

The XPS analysis of DCQDs is presented in Fig. 3 , providing information about the elemental composition according to the survey spectrum. The chemical composition of DCQDs contains mainly C 1s (88.42%) and O 1s (14.33%) with traces of N 1s, Si 2p and S 2p. The C 1s spectrum of DCQDs is provided in Fig. 3(B) and referring to three binding energy peaks at 288.8, 286.0, and 284.3 eV, which are corresponding respectively to O−C−O, C−O/C−N, and C−C/C-H [34]. The deconvoluted spectrum of O 1s in Fig. 3(C) shows two peaks at 532.1 and 533.1 eV, which are credited to C−O and H−C−O [35]. This analysis provides a quantitative overview of the elemental composition, highlighting the significant presence of carbon and oxygen in DCQDs. The diverse elemental composition of DCQDs refers also to existence of nitrogen, and sulfur elements as incorporated heteroatoms into DCQDs, likely originating from the biomass precursor. Understanding the elemental composition is crucial for elucidating the potential properties and applications of DCQDs, especially in the context of their role as corrosion inhibitors for carbon steel surfaces [36].

Fig. 4 shows the XRD patterns of steel and coated steel surfaces in absence and presence of 100 mg/L or 400 mg/L DCQDs after treatment with 1.0 M-HCl for 1 h. Fig. 4(A) shows the related peaks to iron at 2θ = 25.1°, 49.3°, and 64.6°, while the peaks at 2θ = 25.1°, 49.3°, and 64.6° confirm the contamination of steel with iron oxides (Fe2O3 and FeOOH) as steel corrosion products. Fig. 4(B) is assigned to the steel with PPC coat showing the related peaks to Mn3(PO4)4, iron and iron oxide due to appeared corrosion. Fig. 4(C) and (D) are ascribed to steel coated with PPC coat in presence of 100 mg/L and 400 mg/L of DCQDs, respectively. The peaks corresponding to iron oxides were disappeared, while a new carbon peak at 2θ = 20° was observed to denote to the successful DCQDs adsorption on the steel surface to prevent the corrosion [37,38].

Fig. 5 shows the full XPS survey spectra of coated steel, coated steel treated with 1.0M-HCl for 1 h, coated steel with 100 mg/L DCQDs treated with 1.0M-HCl for 1 h, and coated steel with 400 mg/L DCQDs treated with 1.0M-HCl for 1 h. Fig. 5(A) shows the spectrum of free coated steel in which six different peaks are detected at 156.2, 179.6, 488.7, 561.3, 766.8, and 998.6 eV. These are related to the existing elements in the coat surface and their chemical states. After treatment with 1.0 M-HCl for 1 h, Fig. 4 (B), refers to a new generated peak at 212.2 eV for the Cl2p state of chloride to outline that the generated Cl− ions from HCl were combined with the coating to obtain a corrosion products layer. Fig. 5(C) and (D) represent the spectra of coated steel with 100 mg/L or 400 mg/L DCQDs treated with 1.0 M-HCl for 1 h, in which three new peaks at 197.4, 300 and 400 eV are evident for C1s, C12p, and N 1s to affirm the incorporation of DCQDs molecules onto the coating surface. Moreover, the intensities of P, K, and Mn peaks were increased to suggest an effective enhancement in the protection efficiency. Finally, the decrease of intensities of Cl− peak is mainly interpreted on the bases of hindrance of Cl− ions adsorption due to successful high surface adsorbability of DCQDs. Therefore, the XPS results reveal that the assembled DCQDs material is more effective in enhancing the coating protection efficiency [39,40].

Fig. 6 refers to the TEM of (A) coated steel, (B) coated steel with 100 mg/L DCQDs, and (C) coated steel with 400 mg/L DCQDs and all were measured after treatment with 1.0 M-HCl for 1h. It is evident that coated steel (A) exhibited significant porosity in DCQDs absence. Upon progressive using of DCQDs, (B, C), the coating layer was turned more dense and the coat porosity was minimized. It is evident that the DCQDs deposited layers were able to minimize the number of pores and thus leading to good surface uniformity as characterized from the TEM images [41,42].

Fig. 7 shows the EDX characterizations of coated steel, coated steel with 100 mg/L DCQDs, and coated steel with 400 mg/L DCQDs and all were measured after treatment with 1.0 M-HCl for 1h. In absence of DCQDs, it is clear that the related samples to steel coated with PPC (A) revealed the presence of carbon with 0.33%. However, as illustrated in Fig. (B) and (C), the samples with 100 mg/L and 400 mg/L DCQDs were identified by increased carbon contents providing 3.37 and 12.47%, respectively [16,17]. These results support the effective adsorption of DCQDs on the steel surface to efficiently retard and inhibit corrosion reactions.

The thicknesses of prepared permanganate-phosphate-coated steel samples in presence of different concentrations of DCQDs in the coating bath are shown in Table 2 . The sample thickness was clearly dropped as the concentration of DCQDs was raised, demonstrating the effectiveness of DCQDs in enhancing the coat characteristics and making the coat less porous and more adhering to the metal surface. This suggests that DCQDs is more effective due to strong adsorption on the metal surface.

The hardness of bare steel and the coated steel was evaluated to understand the influence of DCQDs on the mechanical properties of PPC. The data presented in Table 2 reveals a significant enhancement in hardness with the incorporation of DCQDs. The uncoated steel substrate exhibited the lowest hardness, while the PPC coated steel showed a remarkable increased hardness. The PPC coated steel containing 400 mg/L DCQDs exhibited the highest hardness. This notable improvement in hardness could be attributed to the uniform distribution of DCQDs within the PPC matrix, which likely enhances the coat resistance to deformation. The homogenous dispersion of DCQDs, as confirmed by TEM analysis, may facilitate the formation of a more compact and reinforced microstructure, resulting in increased hardness.

Adhesion strength is a critical parameter for the performance of protective coatings. The average adhesion strength values are reported in Table 3 to demonstrate the effect of DCQDs on the bond strength between the PPC and the steel substrate. The PPC coated steel exhibited an average adhesion strength of 44.12 ± 2 MPa. This value is higher than the adhesion strength reported by Joseph Raj Xavier et. al.[43,44] for nanocomposite films before and after immersion in seawater for several hours [2.8 – 15.8 MPa]. The PPC coated steel containing 400 mg/L DCQDs showed a substantial increase to 62.31 ± 2 MPa. The increase in adhesion strength can be explained by the improved interfacial bonding facilitated by the DCQDs.

Tensile strength testing can evaluate the coating ability to withstand tensile forces without failure. The average tensile strength values are compiled in Table 3 to highlighting the mechanical robustness imparted by DCQDs. The PPC coated steel showed an average tensile strength of 52.31 ± 2 MPa. The PPC coated steel containing 400 mg/L DCQDs exhibited a significant increase to 75.87 ± 2 MPa. The increase in tensile strength with the addition of DCQDs suggests that the DCQDs act as reinforcement within the PPC matrix. So, the incorporation of DCQDs contributes to a more stable and structurally integrated coat, capable of withstanding higher tensile stresses.

The protection efficiency (% P) is computed from the following equation [8].
(1) %P = [(Rct –Rct o) / Rct] x 100
Where Rct and Rct o represent the charge transfer resistances in and out of DCQDs, respectively.

Table 4 compiles the electrochemical impedance parameters of coated steel with different DCQDs concentrations. The results indicate that as the concentration of DCQDs increases, the Rs increases, and the capacity of the double-layer (Qdl) decreases mainly due to DCQDs adsorption on the steel surface [30]. In this study, the Rct values are increased with increasing the concentration of DCQDs in the PPC coat from 21 to 819 Ohm.cm2, these results are very close to the results obtained by Qi et.al [48,49]. This can be discussed on the basis that for the PPC coat, the coating is inherently part of the steel substrate and exhibits strong adhesion. This is supported by the collected adhesion results, indicating high coating adhesion. The reason is why our impedance response shows capacitive behavior with semicircles in the Nyquist plots. On the other hand high values of Rct are obtained by Xavier et.al [50–53] for other coats different than conversion coats. It was concluded that the efficiency of protection for coats were enhanced to 97.4% by increasing DCQDs concentration to 400 mg/L. This suggests that higher concentrations of DCQDs contribute to the increased protection efficiency, affirming their positive impact on enhancing the corrosion resistance of PPC coated steel by 1.0 M-HCl [28].

The outlined results indicate that 400 mg/L of DCQDs in the PPC coating bath yields the highest protection efficiency of 97.4%. Beyond this concentration, it was observed that the protection efficiency is roughly constant, likely due to the saturation of adsorption sites on the steel surface.

Fig. 10 displays the PPC coated steel potentiodynamic polarization curves with coats containing different DCQDs concentrations in 1.0 M-HCl. The cathodic/anodic portions of the polarization curves are notably affected to suggest that the added DCQDs to the coating solution primarily retard both reactions at the anodic/cathodic sites of the steel surface and thus inhibiting the corrosion process [7].

The corrosion current density, (icorr), was calculated from the intersection of anodic and cathodic Tafel lines. The generated electrochemical corrosion parameters via potentiodynamic polarization curves, including Ecorr, icorr, βa, and βc are compiled (Table 3), besides the protection percentage (%P) was computed from the following equation [8].
(2) %P = [(i0 – i) / i0] × 100

Where io is the density of corrosion current without DCQDs, and i is the density of corrosion current in presence of DCQDs. It is evident from the listed data (Table 5 ) that the corrosion current density was decreased, providing a decline in the corrosion rate with increasing the DCQDs concentration in the coating solution. The corrosion potential was shifted to more negative potentials, indicating a tendency for the coated steel to less corrode. The coat protection efficiency (% P) was increased with increasing the amount of DCQDs and the highest protection efficiency was reached at 96.8% by 400 mg/L concentration. The protection efficiency of the coats versus DCQDs concentration using the results obtained from both EIS and potentiodynamic polarization are shown in Fig. 11 . It is clear that the curves by the two techniques provided similar results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coat stability with various immersion periods
ce:section.ce:para: A PPC coated steel sample containing 400 mg/L DCQDs was used to study the effect of immersion time in 1.0 M-HCl on the stability of coat using EIS measurement. The Nyquist plots are given in Fig. 12 . It is evident that the capacitive semicircle size was gradually lowered as immersion duration increased. The previously utilized equivalent circuit (Fig. 9) was used to evaluate these graphs. The electrochemical impedance parameters at different immersion times are summarized in Table 6 . It is clear that the P(%) was slightly decreased from 97.4% to 96.1% after 60 h immersion in 1.0 M-HCl, indicating that the prepared coat have high stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Role of DCQDs in the mechanism of coat formation on the steel surface
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of PPC coat in the acidic coating bath takes place through a series of chemical and electrochemical reactions [16] as shown in Fig. 13 . The first step is the electrochemical dissolution of iron forming a microgalvanic couple and the dissolution of iron produces Fe2+ ions at the microanodes, which are then oxidized to Fe3+ ions by potassium permanganate. Also, at the microcathodes, the hydrogen evolution simultaneously occurs. The second step is the deposition of insoluble salts to form amorphous film of Mn3(PO4)4 and Fe2O3 on the steel surface. The third step is the deposition of the coat via nucleation and crystal growth. In the absence of DCQDs, the dissolution of iron at the microanodes was based on a fast charge transfer reaction giving high concentration of Fe2+ ions in the coating bath. This leads to a high rate of crystal growth over that of nucleation process and in this case the insoluble salts are deposited randomly and rapidly giving porous coat with low protection efficiency. However, in presence of DCQDs in the coating bath, EDX, XRD and XPS techniques indicated that the carbon dots molecules are also adsorbed. This retards the dissolution of iron and the concentration of Fe2+ ions in the coating solution becomes low which cause higher nucleation rate over the crystal growth leading to formation of a uniform and protective film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of DCQDs performance versus other CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Table 7 compiles the maximum protection efficiency of different CQDs for carbon steel corrosion in 1.0 M-HCl, versus the evaluated DCQDs in this work.

ce:list-item.ce:para: A novel, sustainable and ecofriendly DCQDs material was prepared from Damsissa via hydrothermal reaction-microwave irradiation procedure.

The assembled DCQDs material was characterized with nanoparticles in the range from 3 to 4 nm (25%), 4 to 5 nm (35%), and 5 to 6 nm (40%) as well as the related elemental composition to carbon (87.76%), oxygen (5.29%), and nitrogen (2.82%) as the major constituent elements.
The as-synthesized DCQDs were applied as a protective material in the coating bath of permanganate phosphate conversion coat.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization results confirmed the validity of investigated DCQDs in improving the resistance of coated steel to corrosion in HCl solution giving maximum protection percentage 97.4% in presence of 400 mg/L.

The concluded TEM, EDX, XRD and XPS results clarified that the molecules of DCQDs were adsorbed on the steel surface to retard the dissolution of iron based on the low detected concentration of Fe2+ ions in the coating solution causing high nucleation rate over the crystal growth and thus leading to formation of uniform protective film.

ce:section.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.
B.A. Abd-El-Nabey: Formal analysis. Mohamed E. Mahmoud: Supervision. S. El-Housseiny: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
